coredata.dc:title: Surface engineering of phase controlled defective 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx material for significantly enhanced hydrogen evolution under visible-light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Kehui
Yu, Lianqing
Luo, Huihua
Ji, Xiaomeng
Li, Xiang
Zhu, Haifeng
Zhang, Yaping
coredata.dc:description: Design and construction of non-precious metal photocatalysts are necessary to achieve excellent hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance and good stability in photocatalytic water splitting systems. Herein, the phase control and defect engineering are applied to fabricate metallic 1 T-phase molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (1 T-MoS2 QDs) decorated 2D g-C3Nx nanosheets with the feature of nitrogen vacancies (denoted as 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx) for photocatalytic HER. The introduction of N defects facilitates to design electronic and energy band structures of g-C3N4. MoS2 QDs is beneficial to improve the charge carrier transport performances in the composite system. And the synergistic effect of phase control and defect engineering can optimize the reaction dynamics of photocatalytic HER. The resultant 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) shows excellent properties for photocatalytic H2 evolution, with highest H2 generation rate reaching 4.08 mmol g-1h−1. Significantly, the superior H2 production performance of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) are achieved, outperforming the benchmark photocatalyst Pt-loading g-C3Nx (2.69 mmol g-1h−1). In addition, 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) also exhibits high and stable cycling and durability with no significant decay of photocatalytic H2 evolution under continuous 25 h of visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). Meanwhile, the possible HER mechanism is explained based on the electronic and energy band structures effects of the g-C3Nx and the 1 T-MoS2 QDs cocatalyst. This work presents an efficient strategy for tuning the optical properties of nitrogen-based photocatalyst to enhance solar energy capture and conversion.

head.ce:title: Surface engineering of phase controlled defective 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx material for significantly enhanced hydrogen evolution under visible-light irradiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Design and construction of non-precious metal photocatalysts are necessary to achieve excellent hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance and good stability in photocatalytic water splitting systems. Herein, the phase control and defect engineering are applied to fabricate metallic 1 T-phase molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (1 T-MoS2 QDs) decorated 2D g-C3Nx nanosheets with the feature of nitrogen vacancies (denoted as 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx) for photocatalytic HER. The introduction of N defects facilitates to design electronic and energy band structures of g-C3N4. MoS2 QDs is beneficial to improve the charge carrier transport performances in the composite system. And the synergistic effect of phase control and defect engineering can optimize the reaction dynamics of photocatalytic HER. The resultant 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) shows excellent properties for photocatalytic H2 evolution, with highest H2 generation rate reaching 4.08 mmol g-1h−1. Significantly, the superior H2 production performance of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) are achieved, outperforming the benchmark photocatalyst Pt-loading g-C3Nx (2.69 mmol g-1h−1). In addition, 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) also exhibits high and stable cycling and durability with no significant decay of photocatalytic H2 evolution under continuous 25 h of visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). Meanwhile, the possible HER mechanism is explained based on the electronic and energy band structures effects of the g-C3Nx and the 1 T-MoS2 QDs cocatalyst. This work presents an efficient strategy for tuning the optical properties of nitrogen-based photocatalyst to enhance solar energy capture and conversion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen production is one of promising solutions to the current energy crisis and environmental pollution [1–3]. In the last decades, tremendous efforts had been devoted to explore photocatalysts for enhanced water reduction properties [4–6]. Noble metal Pt-based photocatalysts can greatly promote the HER process via suppressing photoinduced electron-holes recombination or reducing the dynamic overpotential [7]. Unfortunately, the high cost and low abundance of Pt metal severely limits its application at a large scale. 2D transition metal dichalcogenides (also known as 2D TMDs, like MoS2) have been regarded as prospective alternatives to Pt noble metal due to their low cost, high intrinsic catalytic performance, tunable phase component and low adsorption free energy of hydrogen (ΔGH) [8–11]. Particularly, MoS2 usually demonstrates two polymorphs, including trigonal semiconductor 2H phase (2H-MoS2) and octahedral coordination metallic phase (1 T-MoS2) [12]. And MoS2 typically presents in the stable 2H phase, which exists an adjustable band gap from 1.3 to 1.9 eV [13]. However, 2H-MoS2 inherently has poor conductivity and low active site density. Studies have proved that 2H-MoS2 is catalytically active only at the edges of MoS2 layer, which confines its HER performance [14].

In contrast to 2H-MoS2, 1 T-MoS2 has excellent physical and chemical properties [15]. Significantly, 1 T-MoS2 reveals an outstanding electronic conductivity about 107 times greater than that of 2H-phase counterparts, which can be attributed to the incompletely occupied Mo 4d orbitals in 1 T-MoS2 [16]. In addition, metallic 1 T-MoS2 is catalytically HER active not only at the edges but also at the basal plane, resulting in a greater reaction kinetics between protons (H+) and electrons on the base [17–19]. Especially, 1 T-MoS2 QDs are of interest because of more unsaturated Mo&S edge atoms and better water solubility compared with monolayered MoS2 [20]. Theoretical researches demonstrated that MoS2 nanomaterial can enhance HER activity by modulating ΔGH [21–22]. Li and co-workers ever reported that the preparation of high-ratio 1 T-MoS2 QDs loading onto CdS nanorods can be used for visible-light photocatalytic HER [23]. Although the significant photocatalytic H2 evolution performance has been achieved, photocorrosion of CdS substrates is still full of serious challenges. Therefore, stable catalyst substrate is essential to maintain high performance of HER.

Since Wang et al. studied two-dimensional metal-free g-C3N4 material in 2009 [24], it has been comprehensively explored for photocatalytic water splitting reactions because of low cost, chemical stability, and suitable thermodynamic potential for H2 generation reactions [25–26]. Recently, 2D g-C3N4 nanosheets have shown larger specific area and better catalytic potential than bulk g-C3N4 [27]. Unfortunately, the unmodified g-C3N4 nanosheets still exhibit poor photocatalytic performance due to low charge separation efficiency. So, plenty of strategies, including engineering defects [28], decorating cocatalysts [29] and compounding with other semiconductors regulation [30], have sprung up to improve the H2 production of g-C3N4. Specifically, defects were introduced to re-design the g-C3N4 electronic structure for high photocatalytic ability [31–33]. These unsaturated sites can provide more reaction sites and facilitate the photocatalytic HER [34–35]. Also, the as-obtained defective g-C3N4 nanosheets are beneficial for enhancing the optical absorption of visible light and optimizing the transfer dynamics of photogenerated carriers. Wen et al. demonstrated that defective g‑C3N4 narrowed the band gap and expanded the visible light absorption range, and its photocatalytic H2 evolution capacity enhanced by about 5 times compared to original g‑C3N4 [36]. Therefore, defective g‑C3Nx owning better carrier transfer kinetics are supposed to be a promising photocatalyst. Inspired by above analysis, 1 T-MoS2 QDs as electron acceptors encapsulated by the defective g-C3Nx nanosheets will greatly boost the two components’ advantages to lower the energy barrier for proton reduction and optimize the HER kinetics.

In this work, nitrogen-deficient g-C3N4 was achieved by KOH-assisted route using urea as raw chemical, and then thinning the bulk g-C3Nx into g-C3Nx nanosheets by microwave N2 plasma CVD technology. Meanwhile, 1 T-MoS2 QDs were introduced to build synergic effect with defective g-C3Nx nanosheets to provide adequate driving force for the photocatalytic H2 evolution. And the microstructure and chemical properties of the as-synthesized 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx composite material were analyzed by a series of chemical characterization techniques. Importantly, density functional theory (DFT) and ΔGH calculations were performed to analyze the electronic structure and photocatalytic activity of the composite system, respectively. Furthermore, a feasible mechanism for photocatalytic H2 production activity was proposed according to the analysis of optical properties and electronic structure. In short, the present study provides a novel surface engineering concept for the potential application of non-precious metal catalysts on photocatalysis.

Plasma technique has emerged as a promising option on nanomaterials modification for photochemical devices. The N2 plasma etches the molecular forces among the layers, and the bulk g-C3Nx is physically exfoliated into ultrathin g-C3Nx nanosheets [37]. The g-C3Nx nanosheets exhibit large specific surface area, low thickness, and relatively rough surface owing to the strong N2 plasma etching effect. The improvement of surface roughness benefits the follow-up deposition of MoS2 QDs and enhances the particle dispersion (Scheme 1 ). The synergistic effect between the modulated g-C3N4 energy band structure by the N defects and phase controlled MoS2 QDs improves the photocatalytic HER activity of the composite system.

The DFT calculations and partial density of states (PDOS) for g-C3N4 and g-C3Nx were executed to comprehend the relationship between N vacancies and the energy band structure. Fig. 1 a and 1d schematically reveal the structure models of g-C3N4 and g-C3Nx with N defects. As depicted in Fig. 1b and 1e, the calculations explain that the band gap value of g-C3Nx reduces to 2.37 eV after introducing N defects in the cell of g-C3N4 (2.79 eV). The conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4 consists of C 2p and N 2p orbitals, while N 2p mainly contributes to valence band (VB), in agreement with the results reported in the literature [38]. In addition, a defective energy level consisting of N 2p and C 2p orbitals appears at around 1.60 eV above VB location [39]. The surface N vacancies can produce inter-band states and narrow the band gap. As a result, the introduction of N vacancies induces a high density of electronic states attached to the Fermi energy level (EF) (Fig. 1c and 1f), which not only facilitates the enhancement of the electrical conductivity for the semiconductor but also increases the electron transfer efficiency [40]. These results are favorable for light absorption, electron separation and transfer, and facilitate the full role of 1 T-MoS2 QDs as trapping energy levels. The DFT results demonstrate that the N vacancies-induced g-C3Nx provides an ideal energy band and electronic environment for proton reduction, and effectively enhances the photocatalytic H2 production reaction.

SEM and TEM were performed to characterize the microstructures and morphologies features of as-obtained samples. In SEM images (Fig. S1 and S2), 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx shows a stacked lamellar morphology with many pores generated during KOH-assisted process. This structure facilitates the shortening of the transfer path of photo-induced charges and accelerates the transfer of electrons. And the surface of the 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3N4 sample without N vacancies is much smoother (Fig. S1). Similarly, as shown in Fig. 2 a, the prepared bulk g-C3Nx displays a porous irregular sheet-like folded porous structure. From Fig. 2b, pure 1 T-MoS2 QDs are modified on the g-C3Nx surface with well-distributed 2–3 nm ultrafine particles. According to Fig. 2c, compared to untreated samples (Fig. 2b), the thickness of the sample has become thinner after exfoliated by N2 plasma while the macrostructure of the pristine sample is retained. The N2 adsorption − desorption isotherms were examined to detect the exfoliated g-C3Nx nanosheets (Fig. 2d and e). The BET surface area for 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx nanosheets (205.93 m2/g) is much higher than bulk 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (123.56 m2/g), demonstrating the successful exfoliation of bulk g-C3Nx. This interesting structure facilitates the exposure of edge unsaturated sites of 1 T-MoS2 QDs that is beneficial for HER photocatalysis. Furthermore, the pore distribution of the composite sample before N2 plasma treatment was characterized by a mesoporous distribution (inset, Fig. d1). It is worth mentioning that the pore size of the composite becomes larger after treatment (inset, Fig. e1). Thus, N2 plasma treatment can not only build layered structures, but also observably enlarge the specific surface area.

The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of Fig. 3 a reveal that the absence of g-C3N4 lattice caused by N deficiencies (the region enclosed by the blue circle). Moreover, the trigonal lattice region (octahedral coordination) of the 1 T phase of MoS2 can be distinctly visualized (Fig. 3b and 3c), proving the existence of the 1 T phase in the MoS2 QDs. Comparatively, the detailed microstructures of 2H-MoS2 also can be discovered (Fig. 3d), explaining the hexagonal lattice region (trigonal prismatic coordination) of the 2H-MoS2 QDs. The ball-stick models in Fig. 3e represent the side view of the monolayer 1 T and 2H phase MoS2. In Fig. S3, the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) reveals the coexistence of C, N, O, Mo and S elements. Furthermore, the HAADF-STEM elemental mapping images for 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (Fig. 3f) further elaborate that the primary elements of Mo, S, C and N are well distributed on the g-C3Nx nanosheets surface. This result also validates the successful modification of MoS2 QDs on g-C3Nx. Furthermore, Raman spectra (Fig. 3g) were carried out to further analyze the detailed phase of MoS2 in 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx. Concretely, three typical vibrational peaks appearing at 145 (J1), 231 (J2), and 371 (J3) cm−1 can be clearly observed in 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx, proving the dominance of the 1 T phase MoS2 [23]. In addition, the Raman peak at about 1343 (d-band) and 1400 (G-band) cm−1 confirm the defect position and sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively [40].

The crystalline phase structure of as-prepared samples was determined by XRD (Fig. 4 a). In Fig. 4a, N-deficient g-C3Nx and 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx nanosheets exhibit significantly different XRD patterns compared to g-C3N4 samples. And two evident diffraction peaks at 2θ = 13.1° and 27.5° can be attributed to the (100) and (002) crystal plane of g-C3N4 (PDF#87–1526) [41]. This result indicates that the as-produced samples have a similar lamellar structure with a strong reflection intensity at 27.5°, which is the interlayer superimposed reflection characteristic of the conjugated aromatic system with an interlayer distance of about 0.324 nm [42]. It is obvious that the (002) diffraction peak location for 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx shifts from 27.5° to 27.8° with a higher angle compared to that of pure g-C3N4. This phenomenon indicates that the KOH-assisted process leads to the partial absence of lattice nitrogen for the g-C3N4 framework, resulting in reduced slightly in the interlamellar stacking distance and an augment in the interlamellar stacking density [43]. This result is consistent with the analysis of HR-TEM images (Fig. 3a). In addition, no diffraction peaks can be distinctly identified for 1 T-MoS2 QDs, even for 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (20 wt%), indicating the small size of 1 T-MoS2 QDs in 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx.

The surface functional groups of the as-obtained samples were verified via FT-IR spectrogram (Fig. 4b and 4c). As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the strong bands at 3000–3500 cm−1 is typical of stretching peak for NH chemical bonds. In comparison, with the absence of N, the intensity of NH stretching vibration gradually tends to decrease. Another significant change is the appearance of a new peak at about 2177 cm−1, conforming to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the –CN (cyano) group (Fig. 4c) [39,44]. These fascinating results suggest that the thermal reaction of KOH and urea reduces the –NH2 group concentration to introduce nitrogen defects and –CN groups in g-C3N4 unit cell (Fig. 4d1). It should be noted that KOH in the molten state releases OH–, which could react with the amine groups of the urea-derived intermediates to form –CN groups during the preparation of g-C3Nx [44–45]. In conjunction with the literature, –CN groups could originate from the deprotonation of –C–NH2 at the apex of the melon framework in g-C3N4 [45]. The peak at about 800–900 cm−1 belongs to the rocking vibration for CH bonds of alkane and effectively explains the existence of conjugated aromatic systems. The sharp peak located at 807 cm−1 is the specific out-of-plane bending vibrations of heptazine rings for g-C3Nx [45]. Additionally, in Fig. 4c, some peaks within the range of 1100 to 1600 cm−1 are classified into the characteristic of stretching manners of CNC heterocycles in g-C3Nx, which is usually related to the stretching vibration of the aromatic ring skeleton [35,43]. Apparently, after the modification of 1 T-MoS2 QDs on the g-C3Nx surface, the bending vibration modes of the heptazine ring in g-C3Nx gradually becomes weaker with the increase of 1 T-MoS2 QDs loading. This phenomenon demonstrates that a molecular interaction force is constructed between 1 T-MoS2 QDs and g-C3Nx, and the heterogeneous interfacial interactions can facilitate the transfer of electrons between the conjugated systems to improve the photocatalytic H2 evolution.

In effect, as presented in Fig. 4d2, H is adsorbed on N atoms, the combination of the H 1 s orbital and the N 2p orbital forms a fully filled binding orbital (σ) and a partially filled antibonding orbital (σ*) [46]. In accordance with molecular orbital hybridization theory, the H-bonds strength depends mainly on the occupancy of σ* orbitals [47]. Thus, the interlayer coupling effect of g-C3Nx leads to a mediated adsorption–desorption process to promote H2 production reaction dynamics.

To investigate the reason for enhanced photocatalytic HER performance of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx, a series of comparative photoelectrochemical characterizations, including DRS, PL, transient photocurrent response and EIS measurements, were performed for pure g-C3N4, g-C3Nx as well as for composite samples. These characterizations can provide valid evidence for determining the separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs in photocatalysts. Noticeably, from Fig. S4, the photoelectrochemical properties of g-C3Nx are superior to those of pristine g-C3N4, which is consistent with the DFT calculations (Fig. 1), so the next discussion focuses on the interaction between defective g-C3Nx and 1 T-MoS2 QDs.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was used to study the optical absorption characteristics of g-C3Nx and 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx hybrid materials. As given in Fig. 5 a, the pure g-C3Nx exhibits an intrinsic absorption edge at about 450 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition in the heptazine framework [30,31]. Apparently, after the introduction of 1 T-MoS2 QDs, the 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx composites all show a wide absorption range of visible light and undergo a noteworthy red-shift trend. This phenomenon illustrates that the modification of 1 T-MoS2 QDs can significantly promote the light absorption ability of the hybrids and enhance its photocatalytic activity. Besides, the band gap energy value of each sample can be estimated by the equation (αhv)1/2 = hv–Eg , where ν, h, α, and Eg are light frequency, Planck’s constant, absorption coefficient, band gap energy (eV), respectively. From Fig. 5b, the calculated Eg of g-C3Nx is around 2.50 eV. And the 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx hybrids show narrower band gap values than g-C3Nx, where the band gaps of 15 wt% and 20 wt% are reduced to 2.04 and 1.90 eV, respectively. It can promote the production and transfer of photogenerated carriers, which is favorable to improve their photocatalytic HER ability.

The presence of N vacancies can tune the electronic structure and band gap, thus affecting the separation process of photogenerated electrons and holes. The PL technique was carried out to further investigate the charge separation process of the composites. In general, lower PL intensity implies a relatively lower recombination rate of photoinduced electrons and holes, which is favorable to improve photocatalytic activity. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, g-C3Nx shows a broad and strong PL signal peak at 475 nm, which is related to the band-band recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes [43]. After the introduction of 1 T-MoS2 QDs, the PL intensity of the composite is significantly reduced. This phenomenon suggests that the 1 T-MoS2 QDs can be used as electronic acceptors due to its superior conductivity, which urges electrons to quickly shift from g-C3Nx to 1 T-MoS2 QDs and effectively inhibits the recombination of electron-hole pairs from g-C3Nx. However, as the content of 1 T-MoS2 QDs increased from 15 to 20 wt%, the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers in 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx composites increased due to the light-shielding effect of the excess co-catalyst. In a word, 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) exhibits the lowest PL emission, implying that it is more favorable to prolong the lifetime of the photoexcited electron-hole carriers, thereby facilitating the progression of the photocatalytic H2 reaction.

The EIS was subjected to explore the interfacial charge transfer resistance of as-synthesized samples, and the relevant results are exhibited in Fig. 6b. As presented in Fig. 6b, the EIS Nyquist plot for 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) shows that its semicircle diameter is smaller than other samples, demonstrating that its charge transfer resistance is the lowest and can effectively improve the separation efficiency of photoinduced electrons and holes. This phenomenon also illustrates that 1 T-MoS2 QDs acts as an electronic receptor to accelerate interface charge transfer. As expected, it can increase the utilization of electrons and significantly improve HER properties. This result is consistent with the PL analysis (Fig. 6a), which indicates that 1 T-MoS2 QDs loading of 15 wt% is favorable for the photocatalytic H2 evolution.

The photocurrent response results are displayed in Fig. 6c, in which the transient state photocurrent is conducted at an on/off light cycle of 50 s. The curves demonstrate that photocurrent response of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) is superior to that of pure g-C3Nx and other composite samples, facilitating the production and transfer of e- and h+ in visible light. Simultaneously, the photocurrent curves are highly steady and repeatable over several cycles.

Based on the MS plots (Fig. S5), the calculated VB and CB location according to the formula EVB = Eg + ECB are exhibited in Fig. 6d. The CB levels of the samples are more negative than the Eɵ of H2/H+, indicating their potential for the reduction of H+ to H2. Notably, as the CB potential is consecutively regulated and gradually shifted to more negative levels, the energy band structure can be specifically tuned to achieve high utilization of visible light as well as high driving power to photocatalytic H2 production.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution activities of the as-obtained samples were evaluated using a mixture of Na2S and Na2SO3 as sacrificial agents under Xe lamp irradiation. Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7b exhibit the H2 production properties of g-C3Nx nanosheets and other photocatalysts growing with different amount of 1 T-MoS2 QDs. As observed in Fig. 7a, the photocatalytic HER performance of g-C3Nx alone is poor of about 0.49 mmol g-1h−1. Upon decorating 1 T-MoS2 QDs to g-C3Nx nanosheets for heterostructures, the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity is markedly enhanced. And as the content of 1 T-MoS2 QDs increases to 15 % wt, the H2 production boosts to a maximum value. Unfortunately, further increasing 1 T-MoS2 QDs loading in the composites decreases the H2 production, which can be attributed to the masking effect of MoS2 QDs. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 7b, 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) exhibits the superior photocatalytic H2 evolution rate (4.08 mmol g-1h−1), about 8 times higher than g-C3Nx nanosheets (0.49 mmol g-1h−1). Such an excellent property also exceeds that of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (5 wt%) (1.16 mmol g-1h−1), 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (10 wt%) (2.41 mmol g-1h−1) and 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (20 wt%) (3.31 mmol g-1h−1). Interestingly, the superior H2 production performance of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) passes the benchmark Pt-loading g-C3Nx (2.69 mmol g-1h−1) (Fig. 7c). This result is because 1 T-MoS2 QDs acting as electron acceptor can accelerate the transfer of photogenerated electrons, which is conducive to enhancing the utilization of electrons on H2 production performance. As expected, 1 T-MoS2 QDs is a prospective non-precious metal co-catalyst for visible-light-driven HER.

As discerned in Fig. 7d, the apparent quantum yield (AQE) of photocatalytic H2 production over the 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) sample can be calculated. The action spectral range of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) matches the UV–visible absorption spectrum. Additionally, the AQY values of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) are approximately 35.5 % (400 nm), 30.6 % (420 nm), 25.8 % (450 nm), 18.8 % (500 nm), 13.9 % (550 nm), 2.33 % (600 nm) and 1.22 % (700 nm), declaring that the 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) photocatalyst displays profitable light utilization ability.

Besides the prominent photocatalytic HER activity, the cycling stability is also a vital factor for the actual practice of photocatalyst. As observed in Fig. 8 a, the H2 production of the optimized 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) sample maintained at approximately 95.5 % after 30 h of 5 cyclic experiments, implying 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) photocatalyst good stability, which can be ascribed to the interfacial effect between the g-C3Nx nanosheets and 1 T-MoS2 QDs. The defective g-C3Nx can donate electrons to 1 T-MoS2 QDs to achieve complete occupation of
d
xy
,
d
xz
,
d
yz
orbitals of 1 T-MoS2 and thus stabilize 1 T-phase MoS2 (Fig. S6). In addition, the photocatalytic HER activity of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) was measured for 25 h in a continuous visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) (Fig. 8b), and no significant decay of photocatalytic H2 evolution is found, which indicates that 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) has excellent durability and practical usage prospects.

To evaluate the real chemical state of the samples in the photocatalytic reaction for further explore the reaction mechanism, the XPS analysis was monitored before and after the photocatalysis (Fig. 9 ). The binding energies is demarcated to C 1 s peak at 284.6 eV, and monochromatic Al Kα is used as an X-ray source. The XPS survey spectrum of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx nanosheets (Fig. 9a).

is composed of C, N, O, Mo and S elements. The C 1 s spectrum of photocatalyst (Fig. 9b) can be deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies of 284.7, 285.1 and 287.2 eV, which can be attributed to CC with sp2 hybrid orbitals, CN bonds, and sp3 hybridization of CN bonds, respectively [28,49–50]. The high XPS peak area of CC demonstrates the existence of aromatic ring in polymer heterocycles [34,51]. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrogram for the samples exhibits three peaks (Fig. 9c), among which the peak appearing at 398.3 eV can be assigned to bicoordinated (NC) nitrogen atoms, the peak at 399.3 eV is attributed to tricoordinated (NC) nitrogen atoms, while the one located at 401.0 eV is specific peak of –NH2 groups [39,44]. Interestingly, the N1s peak of the sample is transferred to higher binding energy after the photocatalytic reaction. Such transfers should be ascribed to the consumption of electrons in the HER process, which may cause by the π-π interactions in the conjugate system. Notably, the surface N/C ratio is calculated to be 0.96 according to the atomic contents from elemental analysis (Table S1 and Fig. S7), which is less than the theoretical standard (1.33) of g-C3N4. This result suggests that the absence of N brought about by the KOH-assisted process [48]. What's more, the three apparent peaks of the O 1 s signal (Fig. 9d) at 530.1, 531.9 and 533.1 eV corresponds to OC, MoOC, OC bonds, respectively [52]. This phenomenon illustrates the successful modification of 1 T-MoS2 QDs on the g-C3Nx nanosheets surface. To make a visual comparison, the Mo 3d spectra of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx nanosheets before and after photocatalysis are displayed in Fig. 9e, the two characteristic peaks of Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 at 233.1 and 236.3 eV belong to 2H-phase MoS2. And the other two prominent peaks located at 232.6 and 235.8 eV are indexing to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 of the 1 T phase of metallic MoS2 [12,53]. Besides, Fig. 9f presents the spectra analysis of S 2p, in which the two peaks centered at 165.0 and 169.7 eV in agreement with S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of 2H phase of MoS2. The other obvious two peaks appeared at 164.1 and 168.4 eV are allocated to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of the 1 T-MoS2, explaining the construction of metallic 1 T-MoS2 QDs [13,54–55]. In view of the above results, the successful preparation of the 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx composites is further illustrated. The XPS results are compliable to the TEM analysis as exhibited in Fig. 3b–d. The atomic Mo:S acquired from Table S1 is about 1:2. And the ratio of 1 T to 2H phase in MoS2 is at 75: 25 calculated from XPS results (Fig. 9e). Noticeably, the Mo 3d and S 2p signal of the composite nanosheets after photocatalysis is shifted toward lower binding energies. This phenomenon is ascribed that the MoS2 QDs acts as an electron acceptor leading to its electron density increases.

The adsorption ability to *H by calculating the hydrogen adsorption Gibbs free energy (ΔGH) of the sample is an efficient way to further verify the photocatalytic properties. From Fig. 10 a, the ΔGH of 2H-MoS2 (1.85 eV) shows a much larger value than that of 1 T-MoS2 (–1.73 eV), which suggests that 2H-MoS2 needs to overcome a higher energy barrier and is not favorable for photocatalysis. Furthermore, the ΔGH from − 1.08 eV in g-C3Nx decreased to − 1.23 eV in g-C3N4 (Fig. S8), suggesting that the introduction of N defects is beneficial to optimize the reaction kinetics of HER. As expected, the incorporation g-C3Nx with 1 T-MoS2 QDs increases ΔGH from − 1.73 eV (1 T-MoS2) to –0.28 eV (1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx), facilitating the desorption process of H*. The catalytic properties of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx displays stronger thermodynamics in contrast to 1 T-MoS2. Meanwhile, the ΔGH value of 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (–0.28 eV) is markedly lower than that of 2H-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (0.72 eV), suggesting that 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx spans a lower energy barrier to promote the photocatalytic H2 evolution. The above analysis demonstrates that combining phase-controlled 1 T-MoS2 QDs with g-C3Nx substrates can enhance HER activity by modulating ΔGH. Fig. 10b and 10c exhibit the electronic density changes upon adsorption of H* of 1 T-MoS2 and 1 T-MoS2@g-C3Nx, where the blue and red region indicate the depletion and accumulation of electrons, respectively. For 1 T-MoS2, H* binds directly to the S sites and shows a positive valence state (0.225 e). In comparison, H* displays a negative valence state (–0.340 e). The construction of the composite system of 1 T-MoS2 and g-C3Nx leads to the weakening of H* adsorption on the catalyst surface, which will significantly facilitate the desorption H* and account for a ΔGH nearly equal to zero [56]. In a word, the synergistic effect of phase control and defect engineering can optimize the reaction dynamics of photocatalytic HER.

Based on the analysis of optical properties, electronic structure, and electron transfer kinetics, a feasible mechanism of HER activity was elaborated. As given in Fig. 11 , the introduction of N defects and 1 T-MoS2 QDs on enhanced photocatalytic water splitting activity can be explained as: The rationally tuned VB and CB levels can produce a band structure that is well optimized for visible light utilization and adequate driving force for the photocatalytic H2 evolution. And the designed electronic structure generates ample unsaturated sites and strengthens interaction between CN layers, leading to effective electron excitation and carrier transport. The synergistic effect of the N defects and 1 T-MoS2 QDs contributes to a significant improvement in the photocatalytic HER performance for the 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx samples.

To be specific, the introduction of the N defects in g-C3N4 nanosheets turns on the heptazine framework, allowing the provision of more unsaturated sites in the conjugated system [55]. This evolution can contribute to the appearance of unpaired electrons and promote the production of photogenerated carriers for photocatalytic HER. Upon light irradiation, note that the electron is excited from the occupied states of VB to the unoccupied states (π* orbitals) of CB due to the presence of unsaturated sites [57–60]. Meanwhile, 1 T-MoS2 can be used as a co-catalyst to effectively capture active sites of *H and photoinduced electrons [54–55]. As a result, the electrons generated on the CB of g-C3Nx can be rapidly transferred to 1 T-MoS2 QDs driven by visible light and reduce H2O into H2. Interestingly, the nanometer-sized 1 T-MoS2 QDs are well modified on the surface of g-C3Nx, allowing photoexcited e- shift from the g-C3Nx to the 1 T-MoS2 QDs within a short path, while offering more reactive sites for H2 generation at the edges and basal plane. Furthermore, the holes (h+) on the VB of g-C3Nx can be quenched by the sacrificial agents (TEOA). As expected, the synergistic effect of unsaturated sites and 1 T-MoS2 QDs leads to improved photocatalytic H2 evolution performance.

To sum up, ultrathin 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx nanosheets with N defects have been successfully synthesized by N2 plasma exfoliation. And N vacancies are successfully introduced into g-C3Nx during KOH-assisted process. The large number of catalytic edge sites exposed by 1 T-MoS2 QDs and the fantastic intrinsic conductivity observably increased photocatalytic H2 rate. Furthermore, excellent stability of H2 yield of optimized 1 T-MoS2 QDs@g-C3Nx (15 wt%) samples was maintained in cycling experiments. As expected, the combining phase-controlled 1 T-MoS2 QDs with g-C3Nx substrates can enhance HER activity by modulating ΔGH. And the phase control and defect engineering can optimize the catalytic kinetics, leading to efficient electronic excitation and improved photogenerated carrier transport. Importantly, this work provides a feasible strategy for optimizing the electronic structure based on non-precious metal catalysts to capture and convert green energy.
